The goal is really to show how ChatGPT can support your learning by helping you ask better questions, organize your thinking, and improve your work over time. So before we go into live demos, I want to give you a simple mental model for prompting. A good prompt usually has three parts: task, context, and expectation. The task is what you want ChatGPT to do. The context is what it needs to know to help you well, and the expectation is what kind of answer you want back. For example, a table, a study plan, a set of practice questions, or feedback in a specific style. Now, this matters because ChatGPT responds to the information you give it. If the request is vague, then the answer will be generic. If the request is specific, the answer becomes much more useful. So you can think of prompting as giving directions. So let's start here with an example. If I write, "Help me study for my circuits exam," ChatGPT can answer, but it has to guess what kind of student I am, what stage I'm at, and what kind of help I need. A stronger version would be, "Help me study for my circuits exam. I'm a second-year engineering student." And even better, "Create a three-week revision plan with daily study goals and topic breakdowns." That version gives ChatGPT a clear task, relevant context, and a specific output format. Now, the point is not that the prompt needs to be long. The point is that they need to be clear enough for ChatGPT to understand what good help looks like. Another useful habit is to ask ChatGPT to help you improve your prompt before you ask it to complete the task. This is something I do every day. So for example, you can say, "Make this prompt clearer and more specific." Or maybe you even say, "Rewrite this prompt for deep research."
